papers.title: Searching for new Hypervelocity Stars with Gaia DR3 and VLT/FORS2 Spectroscopy
papers.author_names: Julio A. Carballo-Bello Pau Ramos Jesús M. Corral-Santana Camila Navarrete Teresa Antoja Felipe Gran Matías Blaña Marcelo D. Mora

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars are unique objects moving through the Milky Way at speeds exceeding the local escape velocity, providing valuable insights into the Galactic gravitational potential and the properties of its central supermassive black hole. The advent of Gaia DR3 offers an unprecedented astrometric precision, enabling the discovery of new hypervelocity stars and facilitating their characterization. This study seeks to identify and characterize hypervelocity star candidates using Gaia DR3 data, focusing on stars lacking radial velocity measurements. Our goal was to estimate the total velocities of these stars and establish their origin within the Galactic framework, if possible. We applied strict selection criteria to Gaia DR3 data, focusing on sources with low parallax uncertainties and high astrometric fidelity. The distributions of the total velocities in the Galactic rest frame were derived and used to identify candidates. Spectroscopic follow-up with VLT/FORS2 provided radial velocity measurements for a subset of these candidates. We evaluated the probabilities of stars exceeding local escape velocities under different Galactic potential models and traced their past orbits to identify possible origins. From Gaia DR3, we identified 149 hypervelocity star candidates with Pesc > 50% of exceeding local escape velocities. Our follow-up spectroscopy for 23 of those sources confirms that the selected targets are traveling at high velocities, with many appearing to escape the Galaxy, depending on the Galactic potential adopted. Our analysis suggests that nearly one-third of the stars may have an extra-Galactic origin. These findings highlight the need for more precise astrometric and spectroscopic data to conclusively determine the origins of hypervelocity stars and improve models of the Galactic potential.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: hypervelocity stars, high-velocity star surveys or candidates

brief.tldr: [research paper] This study identifies 149 hypervelocity star candidates from Gaia DR3 using high-precision astrometry, estimating their total Galactic velocities and orbital origins. By combining astrometric data with VLT/FORS2 spectroscopy for 23 targets, the authors find that most candidates exceed local escape velocities, with nearly one-third suggesting extra-Galactic origins—ruling out a dominant Galactic center origin for unbound HVSs. The results challenge existing models of HVS formation, emphasizing the need for improved Galactic potential models and deeper spectroscopic follow-up.

source_fields.title: Searching for new Hypervelocity Stars with Gaia DR3 and VLT/FORS2 Spectroscopy

source_fields.sections_text: Method: The method proposes a conservative statistical approach to identify hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates by estimating minimum total velocities from Gaia parallaxes and proper motions, accounting for Galactic position-dependent radial velocities and escape velocities, using multivariate Gaussian realizations to assess the likelihood of HVS formation. Results: Our HVS candidates exhibit halo-like kinematics with $ \\nu_T > \\sqrt{2} \\nu_t $, consistent with previous studies, and none originate from the Galactic Center via the Hills mechanism except HVS07, which is near the lower ejection velocity limit, suggesting most HVSs are ejected from regions outside the Galactic center, with only a few dozen showing possible Galactic Center origins. Conclusion: The study finds that most high-velocity stars likely do not originate from the Galactic center, suggesting alternative ejection mechanisms, with some showing evidence of extra-galactic origins, and highlights the need for spectroscopic follow-up to confirm their nature and origins. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to distinguish between HVSs originating from the Galactic center via SMBH interactions and those from satellite galaxy disruptions by leveraging precise orbital dynamics and kinematic data, thereby solving the problem of attributing the origin of high-velocity stars in the Milky Way. Spectroscopic follow-up with FORS2: Spectroscopic follow-up with FORS2 was conducted to derive radial velocities for a subset of high-velocity star candidates, enabling the determination of their full 6D phase-space properties and origin within the Galaxy.

papers.title: Searching for stars ejected from the Galactic Centre in DESI
papers.author_names: Sill Verberne Sergey E. Koposov Elena Maria Rossi Zephyr Penoyre

abstract.text: Dynamical interactions between stars and the super massive black hole Sgr A* at the Galactic Centre (GC) may eject stars into the Galactic halo. While recent fast ejections by Sgr A* have been identified in the form of hypervelocity stars (hundreds to thousands km/s), it is also expected that the stellar halo contains slower stars, ejected over the last few billion years. In this study, we use the first data release of DESI to search for these slower GC ejecta, which are expected to stand out from the stellar halo population for their combined high metallicity (${\\\\rm [Fe/H]}\\\\gtrsim0$) and small values of their vertical angular momentum ($L_Z$), whose distribution should peak at zero. Our search does not yield a detection, but allows us to place an upper limit on the ejection rate of stars from the GC of $\\\\sim2.8\\\\times10^{-3}$ yr$^{-1}$ over the past ~5 Gyr, which is ejection model independent. This implies that our result can be used to put constraints on different ejection models, including that invoking mergers of Sgr A* with other massive black holes in the last last few billion years.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: unbound, escaping, or ejected stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] This study uses DESI DR1 and Gaia DR3 data to search for slow, metal-rich stars ejected from the Galactic Centre via dynamical interactions with Sgr A*, focusing on their near-zero vertical angular momentum (L_Z) and high [Fe/H] as diagnostic signatures. No statistically significant detection is found, yielding an upper limit on the ejection rate of ∼2.8×10⁻³ yr⁻¹ over the last 5 Gyr—model-independent and applicable to various ejection scenarios, including past black hole mergers—providing constraints on the long-term dynamical history of the Galactic Centre.

source_fields.title: Searching for stars ejected from the Galactic Centre in DESI

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The analysis finds no significant contribution of GC ejecta to the stellar population, with a 95% upper limit of 51 stars in the metallicity bin and an inferred ejection rate of at most $2.8 \\times 10^{-3} \\,\\text{yr}^{-1}$, consistent with negligible GC ejecta in the observed halo. Conclusion: This study finds no evidence of Galactic Center ejecta in the stellar halo, placing stringent upper limits on their ejection rate and merger history of Sgr A* over billions of years. Discussion: The analysis finds no overdensity of high-metallicity, low-angular-momentum stars in the Galactic halo using DESI DR1 data, placing an upper limit on the Galactic center ejection rate of ∼2.8×10⁻³ yr⁻¹ over the past 5 Gyr, consistent with existing constraints from the Hills mechanism and showing robustness against ejection model and non-axisymmetric potential assumptions. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to constrain the population of Galactic Centre ejecta in the stellar halo by identifying metal-rich stars with near-zero initial angular momentum, addressing the long-standing problem of poorly understood GC ejecta rates and their contribution to the halo's assembly history. Observations: This section describes the observational data from DESI DR1, specifically the MWS catalogue, using high-quality radial velocity, [Fe/H], and distance measurements with strict data quality cuts and Gaia cross-matches to obtain proper motions. Galactic Centre ejecta: This section describes how stars are ejected from the Galactic Centre via the Hills mechanism, with simulations showing that a population of bound ejecta accumulates in the stellar halo over billions of years, independent of the specific ejection model. Different populations in the $L_{Z} - [\\mathrm{Fe / H}]$ plane: This section identifies that GC ejecta are expected to be metal-rich and have angular momentum centered at $L_Z = 0$, but may overlap with populations like Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus (GSE) and the "Splash" due to shared low $L_Z$ and metallicity, necessitating careful classification to avoid misidentification.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Uses DESI DR1 and Gaia DR3 data to search for ejected stars from Galactic Centre. Analysis is based on observational catalog data (metallicity, angular momentum) to place constraints on ejection rates.

papers.title: The ejection and detectability of high- and hyper-velocity stars by compact object binaries in globular clusters
papers.author_names: Fraser A. Evans Steffani M. Grondin Claire S. Ye Jeremy Webb Alexander Laroche Jo Bovy

abstract.text: The dense cores of Milky Way globular clusters (GCs) play host to a variety of dynamical encounters between stellar objects, which can accelerate stars to velocities high enough to escape the GC. The most extreme examples of these encounters are interactions between single GC stars and binaries including at least one compact object. These interactions can result in ejection velocities of up to several hundred $\\\\mathrm{km \\\\ s^{-1}}$, approaching or even exceeding the escape velocity of the Galaxy itself. In order to study whether these interactions contribute to the Galactic population of hypervelocity stars (stars moving faster than the Galactic escape speed), we combine Monte Carlo $N$-body GC simulations, observations of Galactic GCs, and a particle spray code to generate realistic populations of stars which have escaped from Milky Way GCs following star + compact object binary (S+COB) interactions. We find that over the last 500 Myr, S+COB interactions have likely ejected $\\\\sim$6300 stars from Galactic GCs, of which $839_{-67}^{+70}$ have present-day velocities exceeding $500 \\\\; \\\\mathrm{km \\\\ s^{-1}}$. Using mock photometric observations, we find that $290_{-23}^{+28}$ ejected stars are detectable in Gaia Data Release 3, however, only $1_{-1}^{+2}$ stars faster than $500 \\\\; \\\\mathrm{km \\\\ s^{-1}}$ are detectable. Even so, we show that observational prospects in the upcoming Legacy Survey of Space and Time are more optimistic, and future detected fast extratidal GC stars will serve as a useful probe of GC cores.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The study uses Monte Carlo N-body simulations and particle spray codes to model star ejection from globular clusters via single-star–compact-object-binary (S+COB) dynamical interactions, finding that over 500 Myr, ~6300 stars were ejected, with 839 having velocities >500 km s⁻¹. Only 290 such stars are detectable in Gaia DR3, but future surveys like LSST will improve detection prospects, enabling extratidal GC stars to serve as probes of dense cluster core dynamics and binary evolution.

source_fields.title: The ejection and detectability of high- and hyper-velocity stars by compact object binaries in globular clusters

source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes a method that uses detailed N-body simulations of globular clusters to model the formation and evolution of compact object binaries, then tracks their ejection via dynamical interactions and propagates these ejected stars through the Galaxy to predict their detectability in current and future surveys. Conclusion: S+COB interactions in Milky Way globular clusters are a significant, previously underappreciated source of high-velocity and extratidal stars, with NGC 7099 alone contributing a notable fraction of detectable ejections, and offering a potential pathway for similar ejection rates in other galaxies like M31. Discussion: The study finds that assuming standard structural and stellar mass function parameters leads to an estimated S+COB ejection rate of approximately 10% of stars with velocities ≥500 km/s, though this may represent an upper limit due to uncertainties in core density, stellar mass function, and binary fraction, with heavier ejected stars being more detectable despite reduced numbers. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that identifies hypervelocity stars originating from dynamical ejections in globular cluster cores involving compact object binaries, addressing the long-standing problem of explaining the origin of Milky Way's fastest stars beyond the traditional Hills mechanism. Can We Find S+Cob Stars In Gaia?: While some S+Cob-ejected stars may be detectable in Gaia, significant astrometric uncertainties and limited radial velocity coverage make it currently impractical to reliably identify or associate individual escaped stars with their parent globular clusters. Escaping Stars Ejected By S+Cob Encounters In Globular Clusters: This study finds that S+COB encounters eject approximately 6300 stars from globular clusters over the last 500 Myr, with escaped stars spanning a wide range of masses, distances, and velocities, and showing a strong spatial correlation to their parent clusters only for low ejection velocities. Contributions Of S+Cob Interactions To The Galactic Hypervelocity Star Population: S+Cob interactions in Galactic globular clusters are a significant source of hypervelocity stars with present-day velocities exceeding 500 km/s, which stand out as strong kinematic outliers in the Galactic halo and bulge despite challenges in identification.

title: The ejection and detectability of high- and hyper-velocity stars by compact object binaries in globular

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical/simulation paper. Uses Monte Carlo N-body simulations and particle spray codes to model ejection from globular clusters. No observational stellar data presented.

papers.title: Evolutionary Map of the Universe: Detection and Analysis of the Shell Surrounding the Runaway Wolf-Rayet Star WR16
papers.author_names: A. C. Bradley M. D. Filipović Z. J. Smeaton H. Sano Y. Fukui C. Bordiu S. Cichowolski N. F. H. Tothill R. Z. E. Alsaberi F. Bufano S. Dai Y. A. Gordon A. M. Hopkins T. H. Jarrett B. S. Koribalski S. Lazarević C. J. Riseley G. Rowell M. Sasaki D. Urošević T. Vernstrom

abstract.text: We present the first radio--continuum detection of the circumstellar shell around the well-known WN8 type Wolf-Rayet star WR16 at 943.5\\\\,MHz using the \\\\ac{ASKAP} \\\\ac{EMU} survey. At this frequency, the shell has a measured flux density of 72.2$\\\\pm$7.2\\\\,mJy. Using previous \\\\ac{ATCA} measurements at 2.4, 4.8, and 8.64~GHz, as well as the \\\\ac{EMU} observations of the star itself, we determine a spectral index of $\\\\alpha\\\\,=\\\\,+0.74\\\\pm0.02$, indicating thermal emission. We propose that the shell and star both exhibit thermal emission, supported by the its appearance in near-infrared and H$\\\\alpha$ observations. The latest \\\\textit{Gaia} parallax is used to determine a distance of 2.28$\\\\pm$0.09\\\\,kpc. This star is well-known for its surrounding circular nebulosity, and using the distance and an angular diameter of 8\\\\farcm42, we determine the shell size to be 5.57$\\\\pm$0.22~pc. We use the \\\\textit{Gaia} \\\\acp{PM} of WR16 to determine peculiar velocities of the star as $V_{\\\\alpha}(pec) =$ --45.3$\\\\pm$5.4\\\\,\\\\kms\\\\ and $V_{\\\\delta}(pec) =$ 22.8$\\\\pm$4.7\\\\,\\\\kms, which indicates that the star is moving in a north-west direction, and translates to a peculiar tangential velocity to be 50.7$\\\\pm$6.9\\\\,\\\\kms. We also use these \\\\acp{PM} to determine the shell's origin, estimate an age of $\\\\sim 9500\\\\pm 1300$\\\\,yr, and determine its average expansion velocity to be $280\\\\pm40$\\\\,\\\\kms. This average expansion velocity suggests that the previous transitional phase is a \\\\ac{LBV} phase, rather than a \\\\ac{RSG} phase. We also use the measured flux at 943.5~MHz to determine a mass-loss rate of $1.753\\\\times 10^{-5}~M_\\\\odot~$yr$^{-1}$, and use this to determine a lower-limit on ionising photons of $N_{UV} > 1.406\\\\times 10^{47}~$s$^{-1}$.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Explicitly studies a runaway Wolf-Rayet star with measured peculiar velocities (50.7 km/s) and kinematics analysis of its motion and shell origin.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.journal_name: Publications of the Astronomical Society of Australia
source_fields.sections_text: Data: This section details the integration of multi-wavelength and multi-instrument data—including ASKAP EMU radio observations, Gaia astrometry, NANTEN CO molecular data, and archival optical/infrared and high-energy surveys—to characterize the physical environment and properties of WR16. Results: The distance to WR16 is determined to be $2.28 \\pm 0.09$ kpc, with a peculiar velocity of $V_{\\alpha}(pec) = -45.3 \\pm 5.4 \\, \\mathrm{km} \\, \\mathrm{s}^{-1}$ and $V_{\\delta}(pec) = 22.8 \\pm 4.7 \\, \\mathrm{km} \\, \\mathrm{s}^{-1}$, indicating a northwest motion consistent with the observed nebulosity, and a radio spectral index of $\\alpha = +0.74 \\pm 0.02$, closely matching the canonical value for isothermal stellar winds. Conclusion: This study detects and characterizes the thermal nebulosity and shell around the Wolf-Rayet star WR16, determining its distance, expansion properties, age, and mass-loss rate, with evidence supporting a thermal origin of the emission and a link to its past main-sequence wind-blown bubble. Introduction: This paper presents the first high-sensitivity radio observation of WR16's wind-blown shell and associated CO bubble using the ASKAP EMU survey, providing new insights into the evolutionary mass-loss history and nebular dynamics of WN-type Wolf-Rayet stars through detailed kinematic and structural analysis.

title: Evolutionary Map of the Universe: Detection and Analysis of the Shell Surrounding the Runaway Wolf-Rayet

catalog_assessment.evidence: Presents radio continuum detection and analysis of a single runaway Wolf-Rayet star (WR16). Includes Gaia parallax, proper motions, peculiar velocities, and shell kinematics.

papers.title: Counterculture Stars: Slow and Retrograde Stars with Low-Alpha Disk Abundances
papers.author_names: Carrie Filion Michael S. Petersen Danny Horta Kathryne J. Daniel Madeline Lucey Adrian M. Price-Whelan

abstract.text: The Milky Way is home to a thin disk that can be defined via kinematics and/or elemental abundances. The elemental abundance-defined thin disk, also called the low-alpha disk, is generally thought to be comprised of stars on planar, circular orbits that approximate the circular velocity curve. While this is an apt description for the majority of stars with thin-disk-like abundances, there are a number of interesting exceptions. In this analysis, we identify and investigate $\\\\sim 70$ stars with thin-disk-like abundances and very slow or retrograde Galactocentric azimuthal velocities. These stars could be kinematical outliers of the thin disk or elemental abundance outliers of the halo. Focusing first on the former, we introduce a number of mechanisms that could alter a thin disk orbit and cause the azimuthal velocity to become slow or retrograde. We then determine signatures for each mechanism and assess whether that mechanism is unlikely, plausible, or consistent given each star's reported properties. We find that at least one mechanism is plausible for each star, and the mechanism with the highest number of consistent candidate stars is dynamical ejection from stellar clusters. We next discuss scenarios that could produce halo stars with thin disk abundances, and again identify stars that could be connected to these mechanisms. With this sample we investigate rare processes, such as binary disruption by the central supermassive black hole, while also providing a unique perspective into the chemo-dynamics and structural components of the Milky Way.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: high proper-motion or unusual kinematics LLM confirmed: Focuses on stars with slow/retrograde velocities, explicitly discusses dynamical ejection from clusters as a mechanism, which is relevant to runaway/high-velocity star origins.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Conclusion: This study identifies and analyzes a sample of low-alpha, thin-disk-like stars with slow or retrograde velocities, evaluating six potential mechanisms—dynamical ejection, binary supernovae, Hills ejection, early clumpy disk phase, galactic bar interactions, and Sagittarius dwarf galaxy passage—to explain their anomalous kinematics and abundances, finding that dynamical ejection and Hills ejection are the most plausible with strong candidate stars, while others remain less supported or lack clear observational signatures. Discussion: The dynamical cluster ejection mechanism is the most plausible origin for slow and retrograde stars, despite its low predicted rate, as it yields the highest number of consistent candidates—driven by abundance similarities to globular clusters—while other mechanisms like the Galactic bar or Hills mechanism are largely ruled out or show only weak or inconsistent signatures. Mechanisms: This section evaluates various dynamical mechanisms—such as cluster ejections, binary supernovae, black hole interactions, and galactic structure interactions—that could produce slow or retrograde stars from thin-disk-like orbits, concluding that while some mechanisms can generate such stars, they are rare and predominantly affect high-mass, blue stars, making the production of low-velocity or retrograde red giant stars unlikely. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel investigation into low-alpha, thin-disk-like stars with slow or retrograde kinematics, addressing the problem of identifying and characterizing stars that defy standard thin-disk kinematic expectations—either as extreme disk outliers or halo members—thereby probing the dynamical evolution and origin of such chemodynamically distinct stellar populations. Observational Data: We identify a sample of stars with thin-disk-like abundances and specific kinematic properties using high-quality APOGEE and Gaia data, applying strict spectroscopic and astrometric quality cuts and abundance selection criteria based on two elemental abundance planes to ensure a robust, albeit locally biased, sample of low-alpha, thin-disk stars.

abstract: While this is an apt description for the majority of stars with thin-disk-like abundances, there are a number of interesting exceptions.
In this analysis, we identify and investigate $\\\\sim 70$ stars with thin-disk-like abundances and very slow or retrograde Galactocentric azimuthal velocities.
These stars could be kinematical outliers of the thin disk or elemental abundance outliers of the halo.
We find that at least one mechanism is plausible for each star, and the mechanism with the highest number of consistent candidate stars is dynamical ejection from stellar clusters.
We next discuss scenarios that could produce halo stars with thin disk abundances, and again identify stars that could be connected to these mechanisms.
title: Counterculture Stars: Slow and Retrograde Stars with Low-Alpha Disk Abundances

catalog_assessment.evidence: Identifies and investigates ~70 stars with thin-disk abundances and unusual kinematics. Analysis based on existing observational data (abundances, velocities) to study ejection mechanisms.

papers.title: Fundamental Parameters for Central Stars of 103 Infrared Bowshock Nebulae
papers.author_names: Nikhil Patten Henry A. Kobulnicky Matthew S. Povich Angelica S. Whisnant Sydney Andrews Alexandra Boone Srujan Dandu Naomi Jones S. Nick Justice Dylan Hope Alexander Larsen Ryan McCrory Julia Meredith Maria Renee Meza Alexandra C. Rosenthal William Salazar Alexander R. Sterling Noshin Yesmin Daniel A. Dale

abstract.text: Stellar bowshock nebulae are arcuate shock fronts formed by the interaction of radiation-driven stellar winds and the relative motion of the ambient interstellar material. Stellar bowshock nebulae provide a promising means to measure wind-driven mass loss, independent of other established methods. In this work, we characterize the stellar sources at the center of bowshock nebulae drawn from all-sky catalogs of 24 $\\\\mu$m-selected nebulae. We obtain new, low-resolution blue optical spectra for \\\\numstars~stars and measure stellar parameters temperature \\\\teff, surface gravity \\\\logg, and projected rotational broadening \\\\vsini. We perform additional photometric analysis to measure stellar radius \\\\rstar, luminosity \\\\lstar, and visual-band extinction $A_V$. All but one of our targets are O and early B stars, with temperatures ranging from $T$=16.5--46.8~k\\\\kelvin, gravities $\\\\log g=$2.57--4.60, and \\\\vsini~from $<$100--400~\\\\kms. With the exception of rapid rotator $\\\\zeta$ Oph, bowshock stars do not rotate at or near critical velocities. At least 60 of 103 (60\\\\%) OB bowshock stars are binaries, consistent with the multiplicity fraction of other OB samples. The sample shows a runaway fraction of 23\\\\%, with \\\\numrunaway~stars having $v_{\\\\text{2D}}\\\\geq25$~\\\\kms. Of the 19 runaways, at least 15 ($\\\\geq$79\\\\%) are binaries, favoring dynamical ejection over the binary supernova channel for producing runaways. We provide a comprehensive census of stellar parameters for bowshock stars, useful as a foundation for determining the mass-loss rates for OB-type stars -- one of the single most critical factors in stellar evolution governing the production of neutron stars and black holes.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Paper explicitly studies bowshock stars, reports a runaway fraction of 23%, and analyzes kinematics of runaways with v_2D ≥ 25 km/s. Directly addresses origins and binary nature of runaway stars.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Analysis: This section describes a combination of spectral fitting and Bayesian MCMC analysis to derive precise stellar parameters—temperature, gravity, and rotation—by accounting for both statistical noise and systematic uncertainties in observed spectra. Conclusion: This study establishes reliable stellar parameters for 103 OB bowshock stars, finds a high binary fraction and runaway rate consistent with dynamical ejection via binary interactions, and recommends future research into the properties of dynamically ejected and binary supernova-ejected systems. Discussion: Bowshock stars, including those without apparent binarity, occupy a similar $\\log g$–$v\\sin i$ locus and evolutionary position as Galactic OB stars, with no stars approaching critical rotation except $\\zeta$ Oph, and only a few high-$\\log g$ or rapidly rotating stars—mostly probable binaries—deviating from the main sequence or IACOB population trends. Introduction: The novel contribution of this paper is the development of a new, independent method to measure radiation-driven stellar wind mass-loss rates by analyzing IR bowshock nebulae around OB stars, addressing the long-standing problem of significant uncertainties in mass-loss rate measurements due to clumping and model discrepancies in traditional techniques. Spectroscopic Observations: Spectroscopic observations of 104 bowshock candidate stars were selected from a larger sample based on observational feasibility, reliable distance estimates, and brightness, with results showing that many distant targets are dimmed by interstellar extinction and some cases of ambiguous central star identification. Properties Of Bowshock Stars: This table presents detailed stellar parameters and properties of bowshock stars, including effective temperature, gravity, luminosity, radius, extinction, and rotational velocity, with flags indicating binary or special classifications.

abstract: With the exception of rapid rotator $\\\\zeta$ Oph, bowshock stars do not rotate at or near critical velocities.
At least 60 of 103 (60\\\\%) OB bowshock stars are binaries, consistent with the multiplicity fraction of other OB samples.
The sample shows a runaway fraction of 23\\\\%, with \\\\numrunaway~stars having $v_{\\\\text{2D}}\\\\geq25$~\\\\kms.

We provide a comprehensive census of stellar parameters for bowshock stars, useful as a foundation for determining the mass-loss rates for OB-type stars -- one of the single most critical factors in stellar
evolution governing the production of neutron stars and black holes.
title: Fundamental Parameters for Central Stars of 103 Infrared Bowshock Nebulae

catalog_assessment.evidence: Presents fundamental parameters for 103 bowshock nebula central stars. Includes new spectroscopic observations, stellar parameters, and identifies 19 runaway stars with measured 2D velocities.
